text: The surgeon selects the falciform fat pad, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a generous opening in the posterior rectus sheath, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon has completed dissection in the left retro-rectus space and is now at the crossover point, with visible tissue separation in the preperitoneal fat layer.

The surgeon identifies the delineation between the patient's linea alba and the contralateral posterior sheath in the superior aspect of the abdominal wall, where the crossover is performed easily due to the small diastasis.
The surgeon meticulously removes tissue from the fascia and extends the incision, ensuring a clear separation between the fascia and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon extends the incision and focuses on developing the retromuscular dissection on the contralateral side, carefully separating the muscle from underlying structures.
The surgeon performs a broad dissection in anticipation of encountering the hernia sac associated with the M2 hernia.
The surgeon encounters the hernia sac during dissection, revealing its appearance and location, which differs from the preoperative CT scan but aligns with the bedside examination findings.
The surgeon bluntly dissects a thin hernia sac, inadvertently creating a hole in the sac during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the procedure, having developed sufficient space in the surgical field to maintain clear visualization as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, revealing a previously placed mesh that is poorly integrated into the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon decides to explant the previously placed mesh, which is visible and poorly integrated within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes a bridge of intact fascia, preparing to address the M-III hernia identified on CT scans.
The surgeon removes the poorly integrated mesh, which is easily extracted from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon encounters significant scar tissue in the retromuscular space due to a prior procedure using the iPump Plus technique. The upcoming dissection in the contralateral retro-rectal space is anticipated to be more challenging than usual.
The surgeon identifies a band of tissue likely related to a prior trans-fascial stitch and works to locate an avascular plane within the surgical field.
The surgeon enters an untouched plane, removing tissue and pushing aside fat while preserving the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon unifies the inferior dissection, extending it approximately four to five centimeters and continuing to the pubis level during the open procedure.
The surgeon identifies the transversus abdominis muscle and notes the presence of medial perforators. The dissection remains medial to these perforators, possibly more medial than initially intended.
The surgeon takes down the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique and systematically transects the transversus abdominis muscle, exposing the transversalis fascia and posterior sheath.
The surgeon clearly identifies the posterior lamella and proceeds to transect the transversus abdominis muscle, ensuring to stay medial to the visible bundles of medial perforators.

The surgeon dissects the muscle from the transversalis fascia, creating a retromuscular pocket. Scar tissue from previous operations is visible, and some dissection extends into the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon highlights the aponeurotic components of the inferior transversus abdominis while performing cave techniques, staying medial to the visible muscle bundles.

The surgeon progresses with the dissection, revealing the rectus muscle and the layer of fat over the neurovascular bundles. The transected transversus abdominis muscle is visible, and the lateral dissection is further developed.

The surgeon demonstrates the initiation of a transverse abdominis release using robotic assistance, creating a healthy space that is partially retromuscular and partially preperitoneal. The dissection is shown to be lateral, with potential for further lateral extension upon conversion to open surgery.
